postTitle: Speed vs smarts for coding agents?
comment: depends on the task honestly. for quik refactors or boilerplate i'll take speed every time. anything that needs architectural decisions or debugging edge cases, i want the smarter model even if it's slower.

postTitle: Speed vs smarts for coding agents?
comment: i've been bouncing between this a lot lately started using faster models for first passes on features then switching to slower ones when i hit something weird. the context switching is annoying but my wallet appreciates it. the real trick is knowing when you're about to waste 10 minutes of a smart model's time on something dumb

postTitle: Speed vs smarts for coding agents?
comment: people sleep on how much "speed" includes latency not just tokens/sec. a slightly dumber model that responds in 2 seconds beats waiting 8 seconds for perfect code you're gonna edit anyway we've gotten too precious about squeezing every iq point out of these things.

postTitle: Speed vs smarts for coding agents?
comment:
me: "i need the smartest model for this"
also me: uses it to geenerate csv parsing boilerplate for the 47th time

---

postTitle: your agent can think. it can't remember.
comment: the ephemeral part is smart. most agent demos i've seen break because they're trying to persist state in json files or redis without any real structure

postTitle: your agent can think. it can't remember.
comment: ran into this exact problem last month built a research agent that keppt losing context between runs because i didn't want to spin up infra for every experiment. ended up with a mess of sqlite files everywhere the "unlimited databases" approach actually makes sense for prototyping,you want to test memory patterns without worryinng about cleanup or namespace collisions

postTitle: your agent can think. it can't remember.
comment: counterpoint: agents failing at memory isnt really a database problem. its a retrieval problem. you can give them perfect postgres access annd they'll still forget what matters because they dont know what to query for. context window management and semantic indexing matter way more than storage backend

postTitle: your agent can think. it can't remember.
comment: so we've gone from "just use a vector db" to "just use postgres" to "just use ephemeral postgres". can't wait for ephemeral vector postgres next quarter

postTitle: How I Almost Burned Out Doing Everything “Right”
comment: the philosophical ai esay would've been great but this is actually more useful. burnout sneaks up when you're checking all the productivity boxes but ignoring your actual energy levels.

postTitle: How I Almost Burned Out Doing Everything “Right”
comment: been there. i started tracking which tasks actually moved things forward vs just felt productive. cut my tool switching in half and honestly got more done. i've been using megalllm to consolidate api calls instead of juggling openai/anthropic/etc separately fewer tabs open = less mentla overhad. sometimes "doing it right" means doing less

postTitle: How I Almost Burned Out Doing Everything “Right”
comment: hot take: doing everything "right" is usually code for doing everything other people said worked for them your system should feel sustainable, not like you're constantly behind on some invisible checklist. if the method exhausts you it's not the right method.

postTitle: How I Almost Burned Out Doing Everything “Right”
comment: thee real burnout is reading productivity posts while procrastinating on actual work. meta-exhaustion unlocked.

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: nice the best projects always start with scratching your own itch. curious what made you finally pull the trigger on building it vs using existing tools?

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: i've tried building something similar twice and both times abandoned it because keeping track of application state across different platforms is surprisingly annoying. emails, portas, spreadsheets, recruiters who ghost... it's like herding cats. if you figured out a clean way to handle alll that chaos, you might have something real here.

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: job hunting tools are interesting because the problem isnt really technical, its that applying to jobs sucks emotionally and no amount of automation fixes that part. still useful to have everything organized though, especially if you're doing volume applications.

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: "finally" is doing a lot of work in that title we've all been there 2am three energy drinks deep convinced this is the one that'll change everything hope you wake up still excited about it

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: nice! the best tols are always the ones you build when you're fed up with everything else. hope it helps you land something good.

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: i've been down this rabbit hole before. ended up with a spreadsheet that tracked applications a notes doc for company research and three different browser tabs for networking contacts. building something that actually consolidates all that chaos sounds like a real win. curious how you're handling the follow-up reminders part that's always where i fall off

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: the hardest part isn't buliding the tool it's actually using it consistently when you're burned out from applications i had a whole system once and it lasted like two weeks before i went back to chaotic email searches and "wait did i apply there already?"

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: nothing says "hire me" like building a job tracker at 2am instead of sleeping. teh real job hunt was the side projects we made along the way.

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: congrats on shipping! nothing beats that feeling of scrattching your own itch and actually finishing something before midnight hits

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: i've built like three joob tracking things over the years and always end up back in spreadsheets somehow. the hard part isn't tracking applications it's keeping up the momentum wwhen you're 50 rejections deep. curious what made this one stick for you compared to existing tools

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: the real test is whether you'll still use it in two weeks or if it becomes another side project graveyard entry. most job tools nail teh tracking part but fall apart on the daily habit formation side

postTitle: The Ultimate Job Finding-Management Tool
comment: "i need to go to bed but first lemme deploy to prod" is the most relatable energy in softwrae development

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: the focus mode toggle is probably my favorite detail. once you notice sites that have it vs sites that don't you can't unsee the difference

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: i've been using megallm for a side project and spent way too long on the loading states turns out people actually notice when spinners feel intentional vs just slapped on the polish compounds good skeleton screens make the api latency feel faster even when it's not

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: honeslty i think we overindex on these details sometimes. seen plenty of "unfinished" sites crush it because the core experience was solid. the trust sigals matter, sure, but a snappy product with rough edges beats a polished site that loads slow or confuses users.

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: my websittes are never finished they're just publicly deployed betas with commitment issues

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: the "favicon that actually works on all devices" is such a small hill to die on but i will absolutely die on it. that and proper focus states.

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: i used to ship stuff without thinking about loading states or empty states. then i watched someone on a slow connection try to use my app and just... sstare at a blank screen for 8 secons wondering if it broke. now i'm paranoid about skeleton loaders and at least some kinda "hey we're loading" indicator. those little gaps between "it works" and "it feels done" are where you either keep users or lose them quietly.

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: honestly tho some of this polish can become procrastination in disguise i've seen devs spend three days perfecting hover animations on a landing page that gets 40 visitrs a month sometimes shipping the working version and iterating based on actual user feedback beats guesisng what "finished" means in a vacuum

postTitle: The Small Details That Make a Website Feel Finished (And Quietly Improve Accessibility, Performance, and Trust)
comment: my website feels finished when i stop getting texts from my designer friend asking why the line-height is "like tht"
